ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general jellyfish food product class; no specific entry for jellyfish marinated in fish sauce exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers jellyfish as a seafood category; no specific marinated-in-fish-sauce variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for jellyfish food product or marinated jellyfish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical appetizer serving of jellyfish with fish sauce is approximately 100 g, presented as a small cold dish or starter at a Vietnamese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Jellyfish, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15245) and fish sauce (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 02047) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for jellyfish marinated in fish sauce as a prepared dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw jellyfish (NDB 15245: ~30 kcal, ~6 g protein, ~0.1 g fat, ~0 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with the contribution of fish sauce marinade (USDA SR Legacy NDB 02047: high sodium, negligible macros). The dominant macro profile is that of raw jellyfish; fish sauce adds significant sodium. FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables were also consulted and are consistent with this profile. Nutrient values represent the marinated ready-to-eat product at approximately 80% jellyfish and 20% fish sauce by weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood products — analogous estimate based on raw jellyfish and fish sauce composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jellyfish with fish sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Jellyfish is a very-low-carbohydrate, high-water, high-protein seafood product with approximately 0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. By analogy with other very-low-carbohydrate seafood and protein-dominant foods (GI typically 0–10, classified low), a GI of 5 is assigned. Glycemic load per 100 g serving is effectively 0.0 due to negligible available carbohydrate. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification framework for foods with less than 1 g available carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from fish sauce marinade (~1050 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid jellyfish with fish sauce due to its very high sodium content (~1050 mg per 100 g), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; a single 100 g serving provides approximately 46% of the WHO-recommended 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and very low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jellyfish with fish sauce is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in terms of glycemic impact, as it contains only approximately 0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and has a very low glycemic index (~5), resulting in a negligible glycemic load; however, the high sodium content from fish sauce should be monitored given the elevated cardiovascular risk in this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium content from fish sauce increases cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with jellyfish with fish sauce due to its very high sodium content (~1050 mg per 100 g), which is associated with increased blood pressure and adverse cardiovascular outcomes; the low saturated fat content (~0.02 g per 100 g) is favourable, but the sodium burden outweighs this benefit for most cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jellyfish with fish sauce (Vietnamese: Sứa ngâm mắm) is a traditional Vietnamese cold appetizer or condiment dish consisting of processed jellyfish marinated in fish sauce, typically combined with garlic, chilli, lime juice, and sugar. The jellyfish used is most commonly the cannonball jellyfish or moon jellyfish, which is processed by salting and drying to remove excess water before being rehydrated and marinated. The texture of the prepared jellyfish is characteristically crunchy, gelatinous, and slightly chewy, with a translucent appearance. The flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, and mildly pungent from the fish sauce, balanced by the acidity of lime and the heat of chilli. This dish is served as a starter or side dish in Vietnamese cuisine and is popular in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, such as at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. Nutritionally, jellyfish with fish sauce is very low in calories (~28 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat (~0.1 g), and low in carbohydrates (~0.5 g), making it glycemically negligible. It provides a moderate amount of protein (~5.5 g per 100 g) primarily from the jellyfish collagen. The dominant nutritional concern is its very high sodium content (~1050 mg per 100 g) derived from the fish sauce marinade, which makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without careful portion control. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 5 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is effectively zero. This dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for jellyfish marinated in fish sauce as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for raw jellyfish (NDB 15245) and fish sauce (NDB 02047), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data. The macro profile is well-supported by the raw ingredient data; however, the exact marinade ratio and preparation method introduce uncertainty, particularly for sodium. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy raw jellyfish (NDB 15245) and fish sauce (NDB 02047) data, as no single approved-source entry exists for this prepared dish. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables are consistent with this profile. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: jellyfish contains no dietary fibre as it is an invertebrate marine animal with no plant cell wall material; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw jellyfish and fish sauce. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 7. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 9. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 10. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw jellyfish. 11. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with very-low-carbohydrate seafood products; no direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this dish. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 due to negligible available carbohydrate (~0.5 g per 100 g serving). 12. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Fish and seafood' as jellyfish is the primary ingredient and the dish is a seafood preparation. 13. Cuisine has been corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Vietnamese' as this is a Vietnamese dish served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. Multilingual naming follows Singaporean cuisine rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to Vietnamese, given the Singapore serving context. 14. is_halal is set to false as fish sauce may contain non-halal additives depending on production; halal certification status is not confirmed for this restaurant preparation.
